data.title: Figma's Plunge Means The Worst Is Over (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I love a good growth story as much as the next person. It's exciting buying into cutting edge companies doing unique things. But at the same time, as a value investor, I have to be grounded in reality. When I see a business that's priced at astronomical levels, even if growth is astounding, it becomes necessary to take a bearish approach. That's what I did in early August shortly after Figma (FIG) became a publicly traded business. This software leader that focuses on its own ecosystem that is dedicated to helping people ideate, visualize, build, and ship out their own creative concepts, is fascinating to me. But immediately upon going public, the stock skyrocketed to levels that I believed were unsustainable. I ultimately decided to take a bearish approach, rating the business a ‘sell’ even while acknowledging that financial performance would likely continue improving from that point on. And since then, I have been validated in my thinking.

Right now, the stock is down an astounding 72.7% since my article was published. That is far worse than the 5.3% drop that the S&P 500 has experienced over the same window of time. You would think, based on this, that the company is suffering from a fundamental standpoint. But in fact, things have never been better on that front. Although some of the financial performance by the business has been a bit lumpy, revenue and cash flows are looking better than they ever have. The firm has no debt on its books and enjoys a net cash position of $1.61 billion. Unfortunately, shares of the business are still very pricey. But with the progress management has made from a product launch perspective, and with the big decline in share price that we have seen, I think that upgrading this to a speculative ‘hold’ would be wholly appropriate right now.

A model for strong returns

As of this writing, the newest data that investors have for Figma cover through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Revenue for that time totaled $274.2 million. That's an increase of 38.1% over the $198.6 million the company reported a year earlier. Just like any software company, a big driver for Figma is its overall user count. But not all users are the same. Some of them, an increasing number in fact, are much more valuable than others.

During the third quarter, the company hit an all-time high in terms of paid customers that generated over $10,000 in annual recurring revenue for the business. That number was 12,910. That's up from the 11,906 reported for the second quarter and compares nicely to the 9,762 reported for the third quarter of 2024. On top of this, the number of paid customers generating over $100,000 of annual recurring revenue for the company also ballooned to 1,262. That's up from the 1,119 reported the previous quarter and it represents a big jump over the 876 that the company had in the third quarter of 2024.

A greater user account obviously brings in more revenue for the company. And management has been able to achieve this, even during a time of broader economic uncertainty, by focusing on quality products. In my previous article, I covered multiple products that the company launched, including Figma Design back in 2015, FigJam in 2021, Dev Mode in 2023, and Figma Slides last year. This year, however, the company is truly on fire. In the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year alone, the firm launched over 50 new products. And in fact, so far this year, it has doubled its product portfolio through major offerings like Figma Sites, Figma Make, Figma Buzz, and Figma Draw.

Perhaps the most exciting to me is Figma Make, which operates as an AI powered tool that turns prompts made by users into what management describes as a ‘fully functional prototype’. In the image above, you can see an example of this provided by management. The entire goal here is to empower users to save time and increase the quality of their own work. And in the era of AI, I would argue that Figma is doing a remarkable job of this. The company even works with ChatGPT owner OpenAI to help users visualize things like data flows. This kind of stuff is AI at its best. It's not necessarily about displacing jobs so much as it is about boosting the productivity of users and creating more wealth for workers and society more broadly.

Even though revenue for the company improved, profitability took a heck of a hit. The firm's net loss exploded from $15.6 million to $1.10 billion. But I would argue that net profits really are not terribly significant. For context though, this pain from a profitability standpoint can be attributed to $1.15 billion of stock-based compensation costs that the company incurred, which was far worse than the $947.4 million incurred a year earlier. Of course, that's for the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year, when the firm's net loss worsened from $829.9 million to $1.02 billion. As the first chart in this article illustrates, cash flow metrics for the third quarter of this year alone largely improved, though adjusted operating cash flow remained problematic. And then the chart above, you can see a similar trend when it comes to the first nine months of this year compared to the same time last year.

Management is very hopeful about this year, and I don't blame them. Product launches are bringing in new customers, especially those that generate a lot of revenue for the business. Thanks to this growth, management is adamant that revenue for 2025 will be between $1.04 billion and $1.05 billion. At the midpoint of expectations, we would be looking at a year over year increase for revenue of 39.5%. Unfortunately, we don't really know what to expect on the bottom line. But if we project out results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would anticipate adjusted operating cash flow of about $452.4 million and EBITDA of $201.2 million.

Powered with these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the company is valued. These are still very high multiples, almost certainly higher than what almost any other company should trade at. Having said that, the rapid growth that the company has exhibited is certainly worth some sort of premium. But to illustrate just what kind of premium the market demands, we should take a look at the table below.

In it, you can see with the financial picture and the trading multiple of the company would look like if it grows at an annualized rate of 40% for the next five years. Right now, the adjusted operating cash flow margin is 43.3%. And the EBITDA margin is 19.3%. The reason why there is such a large disparity here is that I don't strip out stock-based compensation from EBITDA. This is a real expense, and if I wanted to look at cash flow instead, I would just stick to adjusted operating cash flow. But I digress. For the purpose of my tables, I assumed that adjusted operating cash flow would be about 45% of revenue while EBITDA in the way that I measured it would be about 25%. As the company expands, these margins will probably grow. So if anything, my model is being a bit conservative.

In that table, you can see that, by 2026, which is right around the corner, the company is trading at multiples that, for its size and growth, are not necessarily absurd. They certainly wouldn't be undervalued, but it's difficult to argue that a high-quality growth name with a strong market position isn't trading at around reasonable levels here. In the table above, I repeated this analysis under the assumption that growth expands to 50% annually, while in the table below I assumed an annualized growth rate of 60%. In these cases, the company doesn't look bad at all, though again it's not undervalued. These multiples are not unreasonable for a business like this, certainly not for one with a large net cash position that gives it additional flexibility and safety in turbulent economic waters.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I remain a bit cautious regarding Figma. However, I won't go so far as to say that I am bearish anymore. The stock has plummeted and management has delivered nicely since my last article. I definitely will not be a buyer of the stock at these prices. But I do think the picture has gotten sufficiently better to justify and upgrade to a speculative ‘hold’.

data.title: MLPA: This 'Energy-Sector Toll Booth' Yields 8% And Is A Compelling Value
data.description:
MLP units have sharply lagged the S&P 500 as we look to close out the year. The S&P 500 SPDR ETF (SPY) is up 17%, dividends included, through Thanksgiving, while the Global X MLP ETF (MLPA) has returned just 6%. Compared to the Energy Select Sector SDPR ETF (XLE), however, MLPA has performed in-line with oil and gas-related shares.

I had a buy rating on MLPA back in the second quarter, and shares have returned about 2% since then, lagging SPY by some 12 percentage points. I reiterate a buy rating today, and will offer a refreshed valuation view and an updated technical take.

MLPA, XLE Lagging SPY YTD

According to the issuer, MLPA seeks to provide investment results that correspond generally to the price and yield performance, before fees and expenses, of the Solactive MLP Infrastructure Index. The fund offers exposure to shares of midstream pipelines and storage facilities firms that have less sensitivity to energy prices compared to integrated oil companies and E&P names. It invests in growth and value stocks of companies across diversified market capitalizations.

MLPA is a small ETF with $1.8 billion in assets under management as of November 26, 2025. That’s unchanged from my June assessment. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 45 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is very high at 7.79%. That’s about 6.4 percentage points above that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been weak lately, earning the product a soft D ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. The fund also has a poor Risk rating, given its concentrated portfolio and price action that can swing wildly with the price of energy products. I’d call out, though, that the trading range over the past eight months has been somewhat tight.

In terms of liquidity, MLPA’s average daily volume is about 230,000 shares, with a 30-day median bid/ask spread of eight basis points. That’s wide enough for me to caution prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market’s open.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar plots in the lower-left section of the style box, indicating its notable weight to small-cap value. Still, a majority of the asset allocation is invested in mid-cap value. With no large-cap or growth access, it’s really MLPA’s opportunity cost (compared to the S&P 500), which could be the most significant risk.

On valuation, MLPA now trades at a low 10.75x price-to-earnings ratio. Pair that with a high long-term EPS growth rate of 11.49%, and the resulting PEG ratio is very attractive at under 1.

MLPA: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector breakdown, MLPA is almost entirely invested in Energy-sector MLP units. It’s an extremely concentrated pool, with the top 10 holdings accounting for more than 90% of the allocation. So, keeping up with both the fundamentals and technicals of the top positions is key. With the Q3 earnings season now largely in the rearview mirror, there may be less single-stock volatility through the remainder of the year.

I’m encouraged by the modest rebound in the total dividend payout by year, even with sagging oil and natural gas prices.

MLPA: Holdings & Dividend Information

As far as seasonality goes, December has posted muted returns, on average, over the past 10 years. Bear in mind that these data are price only, and we actually have seen MLPA turn volatile in the final few weeks of the year when scanning individual December price changes. January, however, has been decidedly bullish since 2015.

MLPA: Bullish January Seasonality

The Technical Take

With a low valuation and seasonality that could turn bullish in a few weeks, MLPA’s technical situation is lackluster. Notice in the chart below that shares are now mired in a modest downtrend, as evidenced by a falling long-term 200-day moving average. In June, I called out MLPA’s loss of momentum, and that has persisted through Black Friday. Take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—it has been ranging in a soft zone between 30 and 60. I’d like to see that hit new highs with a price jump.

For now, resistance is near $49, while support is likely to come into play down at $45 (the post-Liberation Day lows). Now with a high amount of volume by price up to $51, the onus is on the bulls to carry MLPA through notable overhead supply.

MLPA: Below 200dma, Soft RSI Range, $49 Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on MLPA. Technical issues notwithstanding, the fund is attractively priced with a high yield. Low oil prices are a risk, but MLPs generally benefit from net oil and gas demand (not so much prices). They are like the toll booth of the Energy sector.

data.title: YMAX: 70%+ Yield? Don't Fall For The Hype
data.description:
Article Thesis

Covered call ETFs can offer attractive dividends and a good total return outlook at the same time, but that's not always the case. I believe that the YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (YMAX), despite offering a huge dividend yield of more than 70%, is a yield trap -- its total return outlook isn't great, as the price performance is clearly biased to the downside, and dividend cuts are, unfortunately, likely.

Past Coverage

I have written about the YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs here on Seeking Alpha once, in January of this year. I argued that YMAX offered a very high yield at the time, but that this came at a price -- capital erosion. Since then, YMAX has generated a positive return when we include dividends, but its shares have pulled back by more than 30%. On a total return basis, YMAX has also trailed the broad market, with a 12% return versus an 18% return for the S&P 500 (SPY). Double-digit returns in a little less than a year are far from bad, but going for YMAX over a broad market ETF like SPY or a high-quality covered call ETF like QQQI (QQQI), which returned 18% as well, was nevertheless a bad idea.

YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs: Its Main Issue

When we sell covered calls (selling a call option on stocks that we own at the same time), we reduce our upside potential in exchange for receiving upfront cash payments -- that's true for all covered call selling. But different covered call ETFs approach covered call selling very differently -- some sell them aggressively to maximize option premiums and dividends, while others want to leave more room for price appreciation (although price upside is still capped to some degree).

The YieldMax funds basically try to maximize option premiums and dividends by selling covered calls at pretty low strike prices and with short times to expiration. This results in high option premiums on a per-month basis, but means that the upside potential is "very capped" -- due to the low strike prices, there is barely any room for shares to run higher during phases when the market does well. At the same time, the downside isn't capped at all, so when the underlying stocks pull back, the YieldMax ETFs will do so, too. Since this is, more or less, how all the YieldMax funds operate, all of them have pretty high dividend yields but a relatively bad price performance.

YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs, or YMAX, is what I call the "mother ETF" of the YieldMax funds -- it doesn't invest in stocks on which it sells covered calls, but it invests in the other YieldMax ETFs instead. So if you buy into YMAX, you basically buy positions in the other YieldMax funds, as we can see in the following table:

The current top holdings of YMAX are YieldMax HOOD Option Income Strategy ETF (HOOY), a RobinHood Markets (HOOD) covered call ETF, YieldMax CVNA Option Income Strategy ETF (CVNY), a Carvana (CVNA) covered call ETF, as well as covered call ETFs for DraftKings (DKNG), some gold miners, Snowflake (SNOW), and so on. There is no overly high allocation to any of these ETFs, as the biggest position is still below 3% -- we could thus say that YMAX has solid diversification. But many of its top holdings still have a couple of things in common -- the underlyings like HOOD, CVNA, DKNG, etc. are highly volatile and pretty expensive, while in some cases, they aren't even profitable yet (such as DKNG). I think it's thus fair to say that investors who buy YMAX are quite exposed to rather risky underlying investments, despite the diversification -- during market downturns, many of the underlyings could perform quite badly due to their high betas, high valuations, and sometimes bad fundamentals.

These ETFs, in which YMAX invests, offer high dividend yields -- YMAX thus gets a lot of cash flow that it can return to investors. But due to the aggressive approach to covered call option writing from these YieldMax funds, their price performance is very downside-biased -- and if their value declines over time, then YMAX will suffer from a declining net asset value as well. Looking at YMAX's history, we clearly see this play out:

Since its inception, YMAX has seen its value drop by close to half, while the broad market has risen by close to 50% over the same time frame. Due to the high dividend yield that YMAX offers, its total return has been significantly better than its price return, but it still trailed the market substantially on a total return basis, with the S&P 500 offering around one and a half times the total return of YMAX since the latter's inception. The above chart showing the price return of YMAX clearly shows the downside price bias and the problem of the uncapped downside and the capped upside: When the market pulls back, such as this spring, YMAX drops, but it can't recover to the same degree as the broad market following such sell-offs, resulting in permanent capital losses. As YMAX's history shows, the dividend proceeds aren't high enough to offset this problem. With better-constructed ETFs such as QQQI, investors can get a performance that is much closer to what the broad market delivers. The big performance difference is thus, I believe, a YieldMax problem -- it's far less pronounced for some other covered call ETFs.

YMAX: History Of Dividend Cuts Is A Warning Sign

Based on the most recent payouts, YMAX offers a dividend yield of more than 70% right now -- its fact sheet shows a 75% yield, with Seeking Alpha calculating the same. Some investors may think that weak price returns aren't a problem when you get back your principal in a little more than a year -- which, in theory, is correct. But there's a problem with that logic: YMAX's dividend will likely not remain where it is right now. Both YMAX and the YieldMax funds it invests in experience net asset value declines over time, which results in lower proceeds from option premiums, which results in dividend cuts from time to time. Looking at YMAX, we see that it cut the dividend from $0.18 per week at the beginning of this year to $0.10 as of the most recent payment -- there were ups and downs in between, but the trend has clearly been a downward trend. Since the mechanisms that forced this downward trend in the past remain in place today, I think that there's a high chance of future dividend cuts -- I thus do not think that investors will get their principal back over the next one and a half years or so.

Is YMAX A Good Investment?

YMAX has delivered a positive total return since inception, and also since I wrote my last article on the ETF -- but it trailed the broad market's returns substantially over those time frames. When investors go for the very high dividend yields YMAX offers, they pay for it with price declines and a risk of total return underperformance, which is why I think that YMAX isn't overly attractive.

Receiving dividends instead of price returns can be attractive for those who consume the income, but it can also be an issue due to taxes, since dividends are taxed while unrealized price increases are not taxed. All in all, I think there are worse investments than YMAX, but also many better ones, which is why I'm not recommending YMAX: I think that investors shouldn't chase this very high dividend yield -- the price downside and history of dividend cuts suggest that wouldn't be a good idea.

data.title: Embecta Growth Stalls Despite Strong Fiscal 2025 Results (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

Embecta Corp. (EMBC) released its Q4 and fiscal-year 2025 earnings report on November 25.

Investors were quick to react, bidding down the price 7.24% that day and then another 7.44% the following day (November 26). Since then, the price has stabilized, at least for now.

Why the strong negative reaction when there were several positives in the report? Forecasts by both the company, Wall Street analysts, and this analyst see very little—if any—growth ahead in fiscal 2026, which started on October 1.

What Embecta reported

For fiscal year 2025, which ended last September 30, these were some of the key annual results:

  • Revenue: down 3.8% to $1.080 billion.
  • Adjusted revenue: down 3.9% to $1.079 billion (constant currency basis).
  • Net income: $95.4 million, up from $78.3 million in fiscal 2024, an improvement of 21.84%.
  • Earnings per diluted share: $1.62, up from $1.34 in fiscal 2024, a gain of 20.90%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $415.3 million versus $353.4 million in fiscal 2024; an improvement of 17.52%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin: 38.5% compared to 31.4% in fiscal 2024 (up 22.61% in FY 2025).

So, revenue has slipped obviously, by a small margin, but bottom line results were much stronger. Given that net income and EPS have grown so substantially, I might have anticipated a more positive response from investors.

But they were focused on the future, not the past.

Financial guidance

Here’s an overview of Embecta’s fiscal 2026 guidance from the Q4/FY-2025 earnings report:

Turning to specifics, there is little or no revenue recovery, with management expecting it to range from slippage of 0.9% to a gain of 1.1%.

Its bottom-line outlook number is for adjusted earnings per diluted per share, in a range from $2.80 to $3.00. That, too, looks anemic when compared with fiscal 2025’s return of $2.95 per diluted share.

Wall Street analysts also expect earnings to continue flatlining through not only fiscal 2026, but also in 2027 and 2028 as well:

There’s nothing here for growth investors, since the encouraging results of fiscal 2025 will not carry over into fiscal 2026. Nor is there much here for other investors, except income investors. With the falling price, the dividend now yields 4.77%.

Why the weak outlook?

Cannula costs have been a bugbear for several years. As CFO Jake Elguicze explained on the Q4 earnings call, it entered into a 10-year agreement at spinoff with Becton, Dickinson. In that deal, the former parent company would be the sole supplier of cannulas until 2032. He added that Embecta’s gross margin was about 67% at spinoff, and since then has declined to slightly less than 64%. In his words, “the entirety of the decline over those years really came down to just increase[d] cannula costs.”

Further, Elguicze expects the fiscal 2026 operating margin to be 180 basis points lower than 2025’s at the midpoint. Half of that is due to higher cannula costs and the other half is higher R&D costs to identify and research an alternative cannula provider.

Discussing revenue projections for fiscal 2026, the CFO said on the earnings call, “At the high end of our constant currency revenue range, we have factored in modest volume declines within our core injection business, primarily related to syringe declines within the U.S.”

And, it doesn’t help when cost of revenues is trending up while revenue is trending down:

Embecta is holding the line on Selling General & Administrative expenses. They were $331.4 million in fiscal 2025, down from $365.1 million the previous year.

Those are some of the key reasons why fiscal 2026 revenue will stay roughly flat, and of course, lower revenue often leads to bottom-line results. There are structural issues that will hold down revenue, net income, and EPS in fiscal 2026.

On the positive side

Embecta will enjoy lower interest costs in 2026 and beyond. In fiscal 2025, it reduced its indebtedness by about $184.5 million, and in 2026 plans to pay it down by another $150 million.

Heading into fiscal 2027, it will have reduced its debt by $334.5 million in the two previous years. Assuming it pays an average interest rate of 6.0%, that would be a saving of $20.07 million per year. To put that in context, and this is for illustration purposes only, the company had net income of $95.4 million.

CEO Devdatt Kurdikar said in the Q4 earnings release, “By focusing on operational efficiency and executing restructuring, we were able to accelerate debt reduction and decrease our net leverage, creating additional financial flexibility for our goal to invest in growth.”

Embecta also has a longer-term strategy, which began with its spinoff from Becton, Dickinson and Company (BDX). As management put it in the 10-K for fiscal 2024, “In addition to selling pen needles, syringes and safety devices, we seek to promote advances in diabetes care through thought leadership, and engagement with the diabetes community, healthcare providers and other stakeholders. In addition, we intend to continue to explore strategic collaborative partnerships and acquisition opportunities that enable us to accelerate our growth and give us access to innovative technologies, complementary product lines, and new markets.”

The firm has a timed pathway to its second major transition, as shown in its Analyst & Investor Day presentation in May 2025:

And, for some investors, that 4.77% yield could be attractive since they might also expect capital gains in a few years.

The outlook for fiscal 2026 will disappoint many investors, but the longer-term outlook is brighter. How bright will be determined by how well management executes on its strategy.

Execution challenges

Rebranding: Embecta has nearly completed its brand transition in the U.S. and Canada. Next, it will need to roll out the transition globally, which management expects to complete by the end of calendar 2026. It seems the North American transition was successful, i.e. didn’t cost them any customers or business.

GLP-1 strategy: Rolling out its collaboration with pharmaceutical companies, deals in which Embecta pen needles are co-packaged with generic GLP-1 therapies. At the end of fiscal 2025, it was working with “more than 30” potential partners; this year it needs to finalize a significant number of those deals.

At the same time, it is growing its consumer-friendly small packs of pen needles for the Canadian and some European markets. The company expects these partnering initiatives will deliver at least $100 million annually by 2033. Can management make a solid dent in that goal in the next few years?

Discipline and debt reduction: At the end of 2022, spinoff year, the company had $1.598 billion in long-term debt. Each year since then it has paid down some of that, and by the end of fiscal 2025 had reduced that to $1.388 billion. As noted above, Embecta plans to reduce it further in fiscal 2026, assuming it is able to maintain its solid cash flow.

Reviving its pen needle revenue: In fiscal 2025, pen needle revenue slipped by 7.1%, the result of what the company called, in the earnings call, “a confluence of several transitory factors, including advanced distributor ordering in the prior year, lower China revenue and pricing headwinds in certain markets.” In particular, regaining China revenues is within its control and will be a test of management in the year ahead.

Valuation

On its first day of trading, on April 1, 2022, Embecta began trading at $32.50; since then the price has kept drifting down:

As a result of the decline, these ratios suggest the stock is modestly undervalued:

Doing an intrinsic value calculation, using OmniCalculator.com, leads to the same conclusion:

Sources: EPS from fiscal year 2025 earnings report; average growth for the next three years as posted by Wall Street analysts; Moody's Seasoned Aaa Corporate Bond Yield on November 28; and closing price on November 28.

Based on the information here and above, I expect the price will increase 1.0% in the next year. Earnings are expected to be flat, but if the company makes good on its goals, investors could start seeing potential for growth and bid the price up slightly in the second half of fiscal 2026.

Adding 1.0% to the November 28 closing price of $12.76 would provide a one-year price target of $12.89.

When I last assessed Embecta on August 31, I was too optimistic about its ability to get growing again and posted a Strong Buy. I am now downgrading to a Hold, given that I now don’t see much potential for earnings or price increases in fiscal 2026. There has been one other Seeking Alpha analyst rating, a Strong Buy posted on August 28. The Quant system currently has a Hold rating.

Despite one Strong Buy, two Holds, and one Sell, the Wall Street analysts have a one-year target price of $16.67, a 30.69% increase over the November 28 close. There is little consensus on the price, though, with a high estimate of $28.00 and a low estimate of $11.00.

I would argue my estimate is more likely since neither the company, the Wall Street analysts, nor I expect much, if any, earnings growth.

Risk factors

We expect revenue and the bottom lines to be essentially flat in fiscal 2026, yet there is also potential for declines. Risks include poor take-up on its partnering plan, poor execution in its cost savings initiatives, and externally, a recession or other macroeconomic troubles.

Technological or other diabetes care innovations could hurt both its top and bottom line by reducing demand or the profitability of its product portfolio. It has many competitors all trying to gain an edge in this field.

Embecta depends on its intellectual property and proprietary technology for its place in the industry. Breaches of security or missteps by the company could lead to reputational and/or financial harm.

Some 75% of its fiscal 2024 revenue came from pen needles, so any significant problems in that part of its product portfolio could become a serious issue. Similarly, 41% of its fiscal 2024 sales were made to just three companies, and the loss of any one of them would hurt its revenue and profits.

Much of its revenue comes from private and government reimbursement, and those payers are continually trying to reduce their costs. At the same time, consolidation and integration among the payers gives them greater purchasing power and importance.

Conclusion

Investors bid down the price of Embecta Corp. in the two days following the Q4 and fiscal 2025 earnings report, and for an understandable reason. While the quarter and year provided several pieces of positive news, the forecast for fiscal 2026 did not.

It’s now expected to be at least another year before revenue, net income, and earnings start growing again. For now, that means there is no compelling reason to buy now, whether for short or long-term purposes. The only exception to that is for income investors or investors with long time horizons who want to get paid to wait.

I also expect the price to remain flat in the coming year, and that is an important factor in my decision to downgrade my rating to a Hold.

data.title: Mid-America Apartment: Market Rebound Isn't Imminent
data.description:
Shares of Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 17% of their value. The apartment REIT sector has struggled with weak rental inflation amid elevated supply and weak demand; companies like MAA with an outsized Sun Belt exposure have faced even more of this macro pressure. After a significant bull run post-COVID, shares are back to levels reached in 2019, wiping out 6 years of capital appreciation, reflecting the magnitude of the bearishness in this sector. I last covered shares of MAA in August, rating the stock a “hold,” though it has fallen 4% since then, lagging an 8% market rally and suggesting a “sell” would have been merited. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit MAA to see if shares are finally cheap enough to buy.

The macro environment for apartment REITs has been difficult this year, and the rental market has, quite frankly, been disappointing to me in the persistence of its weakness. As you can see below, rents surged post COVID, leading to an increase in construction, and modest decline in rents. The pace of declines had been steadily slowing, and I originally expected rents to flip back into positive territory this year. Instead, over the past few months, we have seen rents turn back lower, a surprising and troubling development.

This deterioration has been surprising because while apartment supply has been elevated, it has been steadily falling as you can see below. With interest rates higher and rents peaking, it became much less attractive to build new apartment buildings, leading to a reduction in activity. All else equal, moderating supply should support a stabilization in rents. The fact rents have weakened speaks to pressure on the demand side, as renters face affordability challenges amid years of elevated inflation. Stricter immigration policies may also be playing a role by reducing the number of households.

That said, supply is clearly still a driver of rental trends. While supply is slowing, years of significant supply have compounded to create localized excesses, pressuring occupancy and pricing power. As you can see, in general, rental inflation is running cooler in the Sun Belt and hotter in legacy markets. Mid-America primarily operates in the Sun Belt. While this region has superior long-term demographics, in the near term, it has been the epicenter of the apartment rental recession given most of the new supply has been added here.

As you can see below, Mid-America operates almost exclusively in the Sun Belt and growth markets. Many of its top markets like Orlando, Tampa, and Austin have seen some of the most aggressive supply buildouts, creating significant pressure on rents and occupancy. Importantly, MAA is well diversified across markets, limiting exposure to any one city or state.

Given these pressures, Mid-America has reported mixed financial results. In its third quarter reported on October 29th, Mid-America earned $2.16, which was a penny below expectations as revenue rose less than 1% to $554 million. Core FFO was down 2% from last year, given weaker rents and margin pressure as costs rise, given normal inflationary trends. Same-store net operating income fell by 1.8% as revenue fell by 0.3% while expenses rose by 2.3%. In Q3, occupancy was a solid 95.6%, which was actually up 10bps from last year.

Blended pricing was up 0.3% from last year. New leases were down 5.2% while renewal leases were up by 4.5%. in general, during market downturns, renewal rates outperform new leases, but at nearly 10%, this divergence is dramatic. Many existing tenants who have been in place for several years still have leases a bit below market levels, creating room to increase rates. Plus, there is meaningful cost and inconvenience to moving, so even if there is a slightly better deal elsewhere, tenants will remain in place if they like the building. Conversely, new tenants are in the process of moving anyway, making them more price sensitive, especially with much of the new supply offering promotions to solve occupancy challenges.

Of course, if MAA pushes renewal rates too hard, there is a risk that it could push existing tenants out of their apartments. That is not happening. In fact, turnover remains low at ~40%, largely reflecting the fact that buying a home is unaffordable, forcing more renters to remain renters for longer. This has essentially brought turnover to record low levels, a key reason occupancy has held up. As a result, I expect the company will continue to be able to raise renewal rates to offset lingering weakness in new leases, keeping blended lease rates essentially flat over the next year.

Net debt to EBITDA is 4.2x, a relatively low level, and MAA has a solid balance sheet, which provides it with meaningful financial flexibility to purchase assets at attractive valuation, like a $96 million Kansas City purchase made during the quarter. To the extent the persistent weakness in rents forces over-leveraged owners to sell assets below construction costs, MAA has the balance sheet capacity to take advantage and add properties. Its debt also has a 6.3 year average maturity, reducing refinancing risk and interest rate sensitivity.

Alongside results, MAA trimmed its full-year guidance, cutting its core FFO midpoint by $0.04 to $8.74, which was $0.05 below the $8.79 consensus at the time. This was primarily due to a 15bp decline in expected same-store property growth, which at the midpoint now points to a very modest decline from last year. Back in August, I was targeting about $8.75 in FFO this year, so results are likely to be pretty much in-line with my expectations.

Looking forward, new starts are running at half of the historic pace, according to management. Completed supply lags starts by ~18 months, given the time to complete and lease-up a building, and the supply dynamics today largely reflect construction decisions made in 2023. As we get into 2027, the extremely tepid construction environment of 2025 will drive supply, which should eventually shift pricing power back towards landlords and away from tenants. This is why I remain constructive on the medium-term outlook for rental inflation, but I view recovery as more clearly a 2027 than a 2026 phenomenon.

At the same time, MAA has paid out ~80% of AFFO to shareholders this year via its 4.5% dividend yield. AFFO excludes maintenance cap-ex and represents the amount of sustainable distributable cash flow, in my view. This is a healthy coverage ratio, providing excess retained cash flow to fund growth projects. As such, I expect modest dividend growth next year even in a likely muted FFO growth environment, as excess coverage provides room to increase the dividend.

Essentially, I believe the rental market downturn is nearing its bottom, but that we will see results bump around current levels over the next year given a muted labor market but diminishing supply. As such, I expect 2026 core FFO to be in the $8.75-$8.90 range, reflecting flattish rents and modest growth from new properties. Given dividend coverage, I expect 2-5% dividend growth. Beyond 2026, I expect trends to shift into landlords’ favor as the market grapples with very little supply in 2027-2028, likely to support 3+% rental inflation (more consistent with the long-term history).

The question then is how to play the stock. With a ~5.8% FFO yield and a 4.5% dividend yield, I would not be a seller here given the bottom for results is likely in. However, with a meaningful upturn in results more likely a 2027 than a 2026 story, this stock will likely require patience. With a 4.5% starting dividend yield, ~3% long-term rental inflation, and 1-2% unit growth, there is an attractive long-term return profile that approaches 10%.

That said, with rents unlikely to accelerate in the near-term, there is no rush to buy, and growth in 2026 and into 2027 is likely to undershoot this long-term algorithm. As such, I remain a “hold” as I do not see a catalyst over the next ~6 months for a meaningful re-rating higher, absent a drop in long-term interest rates. MAA is a suitable hold for income-oriented investors, but I would want to see shares closer to $125 to actively buy, especially when I see other apartment REITs like Camden (CPT) as offering a more attractive valuation.

data.title: PennantPark Investment: Ticking Time Bomb In The Unconsolidated JV
data.description:
I have been bearish on PennantPark Investment Corporation (PNNT) since 'day 1' (back in January 2024), when I opened its SEC filings and noticed a thinly covered dividend with overly aggressive leverage. Counting from that moment, PNNT has contracted by ~15%, but, granted, on a total return basis the returns have landed in positive territory - at around 9%. Frankly, pocketing less than 10% for a 2-year holding period does not sound too compelling (especially, if we contextualize that figure against the compounded inflation).

However, in my most recent piece on PNNT, which was published in February this year, I became very bearish; the title of the article says it all: PennantPark Investment: My Highest Conviction No-Go In The BDC Space.

Since then, PNNT has declined by another 15%, and, as of now, is also down on a total return basis - by about 6%.

I have to say that the Q4, 2025 report (representing the Q3 calendar period) clearly confirmed that my fears or risks that I saw within PNNT's structure are indeed real and material.

Currently, after a decent price correction, PNNT offers a dividend yield of 15.8%, which, in my view, is a mirage. I will explain below why we should not count on it going forward, or at least for the entire 2026. Also, I do not think that PNNT's P/NAV of 0.84x offers a great entry point. The discount is almost exactly the same as the BDC sector average, but the thing is that PNNT should not be considered an average player. PNNT's fundamentals are in worse shape than, say, Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL), which also trades at a similar discount.

Let me explain.

Thesis review

The Q4, 2025 quarter was bad across the board.

The most important metric - core net investment income per share - continued to drop, reaching the $0.15 per share level. The drop was driven by the same headwinds that imposed challenges for almost all BDC players - i.e., spread compression and lower base rates. While PNNT did not have any material write-downs, the presence of aggressive leverage helped magnify these negative effects.

So, what does this mean for the dividend, which, currently, stands at $0.24 per share (quarterly basis)? Nothing good.

The base dividend coverage level is one of the worst in the entire sector - at 62.5%. On top of this, we have to account for ~14% PIK share from total net investment income, which, on a cash basis, makes the base dividend coverage go from bad to worse.

At the same time, the management has provided the following commentary:

Yes. Look, we're -- thanks, Brian. We're constantly evaluating the dividend. We do have substantial spillover that we need to pay out. It's really the question of how and when we do that at the same time as we're working on the equity rotation to try to figure out what the long-term sustainable NII is. So you've got 2 things going on. One is the equity rotation and the paying out of the spillover. And our current plan is to work both of those processes for the next few quarters, see where we land, come up for air and make some decisions.

I read it this way:

  • In the next couple of quarters, PNNT seems to keep the dividend in place in order to extract the embedded spillover income that stands at $0.73 per share.
  • The chances are quite high that after the 'next few quarters' pass, PNNT revises its dividend to something that is more aligned with the core net investment income generation.

However, let's quickly unpack the first bullet.

The extraction of embedded spillover income effectively means that PNNT will have to tap into its balance sheet to source the necessary cash that is required to accommodate the $0.24 dividend. Remember, there is a wide gap between cash core net investment income and the quarterly cash dividend.

Now, given the fact that PNNT's leverage, as of Q4, 2025, is already at one of the highest in the BDC universe - at 1.6x (debt to equity) - the continuation of the current dividend means that the financial risk will simply increase to an even more aggressive level.

Theoretically, we could make an argument that PNNT delivers core net investment income growth, which would close the dividend coverage gap. In my opinion, it is only a theory because of the following:

  • The most recent base rate cut has not been fully reflected in the current financials, meaning that in the next quarter the result will very likely decline.
  • If the Fed makes additional cuts in the near-term - a high probability case - then the direction of travel in PNNT's core net investment income generation will obviously remain negative.
  • PNNT does not have a spare debt capacity to secure growth via portfolio expansion.
  • PNNT does not enjoy a premium over NAV, which limits its potential to secure new equity-driven portfolio growth.
  • To be prudent, we have to always factor in some additional non-accruals.

Having said that, the Management has indicated a quite interesting route for how it aims to reduce leverage, while securing income growth. Namely, PNNT plans to sell $120 to $140 million of its loan investments (assets) to its unconsolidated joint venture. This way, the leverage could go down to between 1.25x and 1.3x, which would be something closer to an acceptable level (albeit, still above sector average and not supportive of debt-driven growth).

And here I have an issue.

The benefit of 'downstreaming' a significant chunk of assets into an unconsolidated JV is that PNNT can eliminate the leverage from its books that is associated with the sold loan investments. Furthermore, since PNNT owns about half of PSLF JV, it can then benefit from the produced income that the sold loan investments generate within that unconsolidated JV.

In a nutshell, it both reduces leverage and increases income. This is the beauty of unconsolidated JVs.

The problem is that the JV itself, which PNNT shares with a private equity house, Pantheon Ventures, is not subject to BDC regulation, which means that there are no strict leverage caps that have to be checked. So, in order to buy loan investments (assets) from PNNT, the JV can attract a huge amount of leverage to, in turn, beef up its return on equity, which is obviously of interest to the PE house. For example, we can easily notice it by looking at the generated NII yield from the JV, which on a last 12-month basis has landed at 17% - in other words, significantly above what BDCs can earn on their books unless we are speaking about really risky cases (e.g., PIK loans for venture stage companies).

The total assets of that JV portfolio amount to $1.3 billion, which is above what PNNT carries on its books. According to the Management's commentary in the Q4 earnings call, the plan is to expand the asset size of the JV even further, to $1.6 billion. Meanwhile, PNNT, by selling parts of its portfolio, will shrink its asset base, thereby rendering the entire business more dependent on the performance of unconsolidated JV, which, as I described above, embodies much greater leverage levels and thus comes with really elevated risk.

The bottom line

For me, PNNT is a no-go due to unsustainable leverage and a much higher risk level than for an average BDC (even though, the discount for PNNT is similar to the sector average).

I would say that PNNT still remains as my highest conviction no-go case in the entire publicly traded BDC universe, at least if we speak about BDCs that are relatively well-known and do not rely on CLO investment income.

data.title: The 7 Biggest S&P 500 Stocks Might Be All We Need.
data.description:
If you get jacked up to research and find single stocks that you think will rise in price over a long period of time, you might not get too much out of this article. Because while I too enjoy finding that next "super stock," I think the low-hanging fruit on that vine has been found for a while.

Oh, there are always single stock winners. But as I've written here recently, I am of the opinion that for the vast majority of investors, the formula is more like this:

Accept the fact of modern markets: algorithms and indexation now dominate the activity. I not only see it over time, but also over intraday time frames. This month alone, there have been several days in which the stock market's major averages rose sharply for part of the day, then fell hard later in the day.

The point is not that there's intraday volatility, as that's nothing new. But given the forces that drive stock prices now versus years ago, finding individual stocks that don't move in sync with the rest of the gang is increasingly difficult.

We now live in an investing era where a small number of giant stocks have crowded out so much of what we used to call "price discovery" that stock picking may still have some value. But only if you don't care about how the indexes do. I never did care too much about them. But when writing to a mass audience, let's just say if you don't, you get friendly with the crickets.

So this article is not about my saying "don't stock pick." It is about providing some rationale for my thesis that stock investing should be done differently. I'm covering that in several articles here, and this one focuses on 8 stocks that I think are like that top wide receiver on a football team you are playing: you have to account for where they are on the field at all times, or your defense will get burned.

These 8 stocks are like a hurricane force, drawing in all of the liquidity. Is it because they are popular? Only in part. The bigger issue is that they ARE the market. They are an unavoidable force that gets in the way of, well, nearly everything. If you are a stock-picker, that is.

Here are what I simply refer to as the "Big 7." Not an especially creative name there. And its really the Magnificent 7 coined by Jim Cramer, but with Broadcom (AVGO) replacing Tesla (TSLA), which I consider to be a bit different that the others. I could throw it in there, but its price behavior is so erratic, and its business model continually being debated. I can't say that about the others. And, it is now behind the other 7 in market cap.

What I'm pointing out here is that I'm increasingly of the opinion that if one is competing in any way with the S&P 500 (SP500) or Nasdaq 100 (NDX) (many professionals do, and many retail investors do as well), it is best to understand just how much "indifferent" capital flows into these 7 stocks. They represent 36% of the entire S&P 500. 7 stocks, out of 500! And even more of the Nasdaq 100.

That means when an S&P 500 index fund brings in another $1 billion...which happens a lot...$360 million goes to these 7 stocks. There's no manager or sentiment directing it. Just the push-button nature of indexing. A self-fulfilling prophesy that has gone on for a generation. Which is why that genie might not go back in the bottle for a long time. Its the new reality. And it has the side effect of choking off value-added stock-picking.

These 7 stocks are different enough in how their prices trend over time, an investor can confine their work to that set of equities and likely be in sync with the broader market. But at the same time, exploit some differences between them over shorter time frames.

I will likely take a try at running a portfolio for myself that allocates among them, in a trader's time frame, while also allowing myself to short them via inverse ETFs. Which they all now have. That's another story for another time.

The Big 7 stocks: analysis

Let's summarize these 7 giant names that have driven the US stock market to its current heights. Because without these, the market would have gone just about nowhere for the past 5 years. Over that time, the S&P 500 has gained a whopping 317 points. That translated to a 15% annualized return, one of the best in history. Those 7 stocks accounted for nearly half of that gain. The other 493 combined mattered as much as 7.

And if they are overvalued because of that, my analysis at this stage of the battle is the rest of the S&P 500 stocks will not provide a sustainable alternative. Not because they aren't great companies. But because the way indexing works, if the big ones are sold off, the others will be too.

It's one market, more than at any time in my career (40 years) - And to not factor that in as a primary part of one's ongoing analysis is a huge mistake

Here they are in summary form. Big beyond what we could have imagined 20 years ago.

I wasn't going to use current ratings in this article, but when I saw that SA Quant had Hold ratings on all 7 of these stocks, I thought it would a must to include it. That analysis process might as well be saying the S&P 500 is at best a low total return proposition for a while. And since 6 of the 7 are a strong buy from Wall Street analyst consensus, that's a nice little divergence we should all keep an eye on into 2026.

Quant factors grades are broken out here. No surprise, the valuation is the issue. Profitability is not in question.

And they have plenty of momentum, but that is backward-looking. So again, a showdown to watch into next year. Will the momentum reverse, or will the valuations continue to be ignored?

The other aspect of this group is that this group can be hedged quite easily. ETFs exist to short the Nasdaq (PSQ), S&P 500 (SH) and even the Mag-7 (QQQD), among others. And as I've written here many times, option collars on these individually are a great application of risk management.

Performance of these 7 stocks: more correlated than it looks

I know its popular to take fixed, snapshot time periods like 1-3-5 year returns and draw conclusions. I don't do too much of that. I prefer to look at it like this: rolling returns, and over fairly short periods.

And when we do that, we see that most of the time, in 3 month intervals, these stocks move largely in unison. Why shouldn't they? They are all being supported by the same factors. That which does not care what the Fed does, or what earnings were. The "passive flow" into index ETFs. Good for now, catastrophic when it reverses.

And that's why I am focused on these market leaders. Because I've looked at many, many stocks, and either at the market, sector or industry level, their price movement most of the time can be tied back to what happens to the biggest stocks in their group. That's another subject I hope to write about here soon. There's a ton of evidence on that.

Yet at the same time, the volatility (think S&P VIX Index (VIX), but for a single stock, not the S&P 500), on each of these stocks will fluctuate wildly. All I'm looking for as an equity investor these days is an EDGE. And the fact that regular gyrations over shorter time frames are enough to trade and allocate among these stocks, that's reason enough for me to be looking into that "Big 7 long-short" portfolio setup I mentioned above.

The exception to this: when the market falls, they all fall. These and most of the other 493 stocks in the S&P 500. THIS is why I always have hedging front-of-mind. Another possible spinoff article. Before this one gets too long.

The takeaway

These 7 stocks are different enough in how their prices trend over time, an investor can confine their work to that set of equities and likely be in sync with the broader market. But at the same time, exploit some differences between them over shorter time frames.

I will likely take a try at running a portfolio for myself that allocates among them, in a trader's time frame, while also allowing myself to short them via inverse ETFs. Which they all now have. That's another story for another time.

I'll look at the technical charts and other aspects of these 7 names over the coming weeks, in preparation for 2026. But to me, if I'm looking to limit the amount of time I allocate to stock selection (and I am, given the limited value it now offers), I start with the Big 7, the head of the market monster, made so by the massive force of indexation.

And if I want to have some fun-for-profit activity looking at other stocks, I'll do that on the side. Not as the main focus.

data.title: Western Midstream: 9.3% Yield With Room To Grow
data.description:
I have covered Western Midstream Partners LP (WES) before, where I outlined the investment thesis in detail and explained why I considered it an interesting buy. Since then, the company has announced the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions for $1.5 billion, which I consider highly positive.

Background

Western Midstream Partners LP is a midstream operator with a strong focus on the gas and water segments, with its main operations in the Delaware Basin.

Moreover, last quarter the company announced the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions, which will further increase the weight of water treatment, a segment that I expect to experience huge growth.

In the Delaware Basin, for every barrel of oil produced, between 4.5 and 5.5 barrels of water are generated, which must be disposed of. Before the Aris acquisition, Western Midstream already had plans to expand its water business with organic growth.

The Pathfinder Pipeline will be a perfect match with the Aris assets and is expected to be in service in Q1 2027.

Moreover, the company is also investing in its gas business with the North Loving II, which will increase Western Midstream’s Delaware Basin processing capacity by more than 10%. The oil business is residual, representing less than 15% of the company’s EBITDA.

As an MLP company, income is passed through to unitholders and represents the primary use of free cash flow, followed by CapEx. Most of this CapEx will be used for expansion to ensure future distribution growth that management targets at mid- to low-single digits.

Moreover, to ensure low volatility and sustainable distributions, most of the company’s business is fee based, with only a small commodity price exposure.

This has allowed the company to increase its distribution over the past several years and continue targeting future growth in the mid- to low-single digits.

Financial Position and Valuation

Western Midstream has a comfortable financial position with $177 million in cash and $6.9 billion in debt. While the debt may seem elevated, it is within the sector standards at less than 3x net leverage; moreover, after paying the 2025 maturities with cash on hand, as commented on in the previous article, near-term maturities are minimal:

Regarding valuation, as an MLP, its yield and how well it is covered are very important.

Western Midstream is one of the highest yield companies in the Russell 3000 with investment grade and the highest-yield company among peers.

I believe that this high yield is a huge opportunity, as the company has good growth prospects and the distribution is well covered with a coverage ratio around 1.12.

Moreover, the Aris acquisition will further increase the coverage ratio and potential future distributions. In the conference call, management stated that Aris will contribute between $45 million and $50 million of adjusted EBITDA over 2.5 months.

Assuming this EBITDA translates to a $30 million FCF increase in a full quarter, the coverage ratio will rise to 1.14 even accounting for the dilution. Taking into consideration the announced synergies, the coverage ratio increases to 1.16.

I believe that the company could trade closer to its historical yield and more in line with peers at an 8% yield, which suggests a fair value of $45.5 per unit, before accounting for a distribution increase next year. Assuming that next year the distribution is increased to $0.95 per unit, the fair value will increase to $47.5 per unit.

Risks

The company’s main risk is a prolonged time of low commodity prices. As mentioned earlier, most of Western Midstream’s business is fee based and commodity prices have very little impact on its results; however, if commodity prices remain low for several quarters, O&G companies will reduce CapEx and volumes will decline. If that situation persists, it will weigh on the company’s performance.

With all the regasification capacity coming online, I expect gas prices to move higher; however, they are also affected by oil.

Another risk is the Occidental (OXY) stake. Occidental is the largest shareholder in Western Midstream and also controls the general partner. Its current stake is around 40% of the common units; however, the company sold shares last year and could do it again if it needs cash in the future.

Conclusion

Western Midstream's acquisition of Aris Water Solutions will allow the company to expand its presence in the water treatment segment while increasing its coverage ratio and providing future growth.

I believe that this acquisition will support the stock in the long run and could help move the stock higher once it is integrated. Meanwhile, the company pays a generous 9.3% yield, the highest among peers, and has some upside potential with my fair value of $47.5 per share.

data.title: How To Position Your Portfolio For A Potential Lost Decade
data.description:
Written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor

Probably every investor who reads financial articles like this one has heard the phrase, "Time in the market is better than timing the market."

And it's true.

The data is unambiguously clear: The longer an investor holds a broadly diversified index of stocks, the higher the likelihood of positive total returns.

For holding periods of a decade or more, an investor is highly likely to generate positive returns.

On the other hand, this data is based on rolling 10-year returns over the last century, during which average stock valuations were significantly lower than they are today.

Although rare, there have been "lost decades" for the stock market in the past, in which the major indices take around a decade or more to retake their previous highs. These have typically happened after asset price bubbles, such as the Great Depression era following the 1920s market bubble, the Japanese market bubble in the 1980s, and the US dot com bubble in the late 1990s.

Of course, bubbles can only be identified with certainty in retrospect, and it is unclear whether the high stock market valuations of today indicate a bubble.

At the very least, we think investors should acknowledge the possibility of an asset price bubble associated with artificial intelligence.

If that possibility does become a reality, then another lost decade for stocks becomes far more likely.

In what follows, we look at the data that points to "lost decade" risk, and then we discuss how to position your portfolio to mitigate its negative effects.

Why "Lost Decade" Risk Is High Right Now

We don't want to come across as alarmist or sensationalist.

Historically, the S&P 500 (SPY) generates positive total returns in the vast majority of rolling 10-year periods.

Here's a chart showing rolling decade returns during the 8.5-decade period since the Great Depression:

The 1930s was an incredibly difficult period, including a high in the unemployment rate reaching 25% early in the decade. Unsurprisingly, stock market returns were mildly negative until World War II began and kicked off a massive amount of fiscal spending.

During the 1970s, there was a 7-year period of rangebound stock prices until interest rates peaked and began declining in the early 1980s. But over 10-year rolling periods, stock market returns remained positive in these decades.

And finally, the 2000s witnessed a true "lost decade" after the popping of the dot com bubble in early 2000.

Although the SPY got close to its 2000 high in 2008, the index did not surpass its prior high until May 2013.

Of course, the dot com bubble was a byproduct of the burgeoning Internet economy, which primarily involved technology companies.

As such, it should not be surprising to find that the tech-heavy Nasdaq index (QQQ) was hit far harder by the bursting of the dot com bubble, shedding 3/4ths of its peak value over the following years.

It took about 17 years for the QQQ to return to its dot com bubble peak from early 2000.

Today, the S&P 500 looks more like the QQQ than it did in 2000. The S&P 500 currently has an over 35% allocation to the technology sector, and its top 10 stocks make up about 40% of the total index. In other words, the S&P 500 is highly exposed to AI-related tech and is more of a growth fund than it ever has been.

To be clear, we are not suggesting a one-to-one parallel between the dot com bubble and today's AI-driven market.

History doesn't repeat, even if it does sometimes "rhyme."

While many of the specifics of each technology-driven stock market boom are largely distinct, it's notable that overall valuations look increasingly similar.

As of the end of October 2025, the SPY reached a forward P/E ratio of about 23x, which is only a little over one point below the forward P/E peak in the late 1990s.

This is almost two standard deviations above the 30-year average forward P/E ratio of about 17x.

Notice also that the SPY's dividend yield is significantly lower than its 30-year average of 2%, and the index's earnings yield is now lower than the average corporate bond yield by 60 basis points compared to its 30-year average premium of 70 basis points.

As for the Shiller cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ("CAPE") ratio, which measures stock prices against trailing 10-year earnings, the current ~40x multiple is well above its 30-year average of 28x and is approaching the dot com bubble high.

It is true that stocks are more deserving of a higher valuation multiple right now because earnings growth is robust.

But it is also true that stock prices and valuations tend to overshoot to both the upside and the downside during big swings.

The higher stock valuations go, the more future earnings growth they are pricing in. The more future earnings that get priced in, the greater the risk that future growth fails to live up the hype.

That's what happened to tech stocks during and after the dot com bubble. Earnings growth for Internet-related companies grew strongly in the 2000s, but valuations simply got too far ahead of themselves at the peak of the bubble, leading to poor stock price performance even as fundamentals got stronger and stronger. Such is the risk of pricing in too much future profit too quickly.

Hence we find this scatterplot chart from JP Morgan, showing forward SPY returns when the index has reached its current forward P/E ratio in the past:

At current valuations, stocks have sometimes been positive and sometimes negative over the next year. But over the next five years, forward annualized returns have always been flat to slightly negative.

Could this time be different? Yes, it could.

But historically, it has almost never been a good idea to bet that this time is different.

Insulate Yourself From "Lost Decade" Risk With The Three D's

The above line of reasoning persuades us that the risk of another lost decade in stock market gains is elevated right now.

It is by no means certain that we will suffer a lost decade. We are not even asserting that it is probable.

But surely the prospect of a lost decade is plausible enough to give investors pause.

So how do investors protect themselves from portfolio stagnation or value erosion through a lost decade?

We think of three suggestions, all conveniently starting with the letter "D."

  1. Discipline
  2. Diversification
  3. Dividends

First, maintain discipline while sticking to your predetermined investment strategy and financial plan. Changing your strategy based on market momentum, whether that means piling into what's going up or panic-selling out of what is falling, is rarely a good idea.

Remember the classic line about "time in the market versus timing the market."

Second, diversify!

While timing the market is generally a bad idea, diversification is almost always a good idea. One might think that by owning a broad-based index like the S&P 500 they are diversified, and to some degree, they are. But recall the point above that 35% of the SPY is in the technology sector and 40% of the index is concentrated in its top 10 holdings. The top 20 stocks currently account for about 50% of the total index.

In other words, the SPY is extremely top-heavy and tied to the fortunes of AI. The Nasdaq index is even more concentrated with over 50% of the index in its top 10 holdings.

This means that if the bulk of one's investment portfolio is in the SPY and QQQ, one is not very diversified.

To what could an investor allocate in order to provide true diversification?

Judging by the dot com bubble (and its aftermath in the years following it), it appears that the energy and real estate sectors are among the best diversifiers during a technology boom.

As you can see above, the Vanguard Energy Fund (VGENX) and the Vanguard Real Estate Fund (VGSIX) significantly underperformed during the Internet euphoria of the late 1990s and into early 2000, but afterward they vastly outperformed the major indices as the bubble deflated.

Given the fact that energy makes up only ~3% and real estate only ~2% of the SPY today, overweighting these two sectors looks like a great way to diversify in today's market environment.

Meanwhile, the sector-diversified Vanguard Equity Income Fund (VEIPX) also outperformed the major indices, even though its modest exposure to the Internet economy prevented it from soaring like the energy and real estate sectors.

Third, overweighting one's portfolio to dividend-paying stocks insulates one from "lost decade" risk in two ways:

  1. It avoids the most expensive and least profitable companies by nature of emphasizing a decent yield and stable dividend payout.
  2. It provides a steady stream of cash into one's account to use for reinvestment (for accumulators) or in lieu of selling shares (for withdrawers).

The ability to reinvest dividends when stock prices are falling is a fantastic way to compound one's portfolio value and total income over time.

And for retirees who are withdrawing funds from their accounts to pay for living expenses, dividends reduce the need to sell shares when stock prices are down.

Bottom Line

After the phenomenal rally in AI-related stocks in recent years to historically high valuations, the risk of a lost decade in the stock market is uncomfortably high today.

But we think that through discipline, diversification, and an emphasis on dividends, investors can largely insulate themselves from this risk.

That's exactly what we aim to do at High Yield Investor.

In fact, the energy sector is one of our favorite places to hunt for attractive buying opportunities today. Specifically, we think the midstream oil & gas sub-sector, exemplified by the Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX) and the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), offers a stellar combination of high yields, dividend growth, and fundamental strength and stability.

In the 2010s, midstream businesses tended to be somewhat overleveraged and vulnerable to commodity price swings. But over the last decade, most of these companies have deleveraged and moved toward self-funding models that fundamentally reduce their cash flow volatility.

As far as AI diversifiers go, we think midstream energy is among the most compelling today.

data.title: It All Could Come Down To Depreciation
data.description:
The AI Revolution has powered most of the gains in the market since Chat GPT made its debut in November of 2022. This rally has resulted in AI related names like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT) now having higher market capitalizations than the annual GDPs of either France or the United Kingdom.

AI is just the latest technology paradigm shift that has played out across the United States during its history, every quarter century or so. The fact that AI buildout is building out a vast network of new infrastructure is very similar to post civil war railroad boom, which was followed by the build out of the electrical grid throughout the country. And, of course, the AI revolution is probably most similar to the birth of the internet in the mid-1990s

All of these paradigm shifts resulted in a huge boost to productivity and raised the standard of living for Americans. Railroads vastly expanded the ability to move goods and at a much lower cost throughout the country. The buildout the electrical grid brought 'modern living' to Americans and enabled mass automobile production and the advent of air conditioning, central heating, radio, television and myriad other businesses and niceties of life.

And the developmental of the internet connected the globe and birthed social media, instant search, and online retailing among numerous new businesses. In addition, all of these paradigm shifts followed similar patterns. A transformational new technology emerges that attracts a massive amount of new capital. A lot of this new capital ends up being misallocated. The new capital spurs a huge surge in spending on the new technology, triggering a large stock market bubble. This bubble eventually pops spectacularly. This typically triggers a large economic contraction. From the Panic of 1873 to the Internet Bust at the beginning of this century.

From the crash, a few champions emerge from the carnage. Think Amazon (AMZN), whose stock lost 90% of its value during the Internet Bust, but has become an online retailing and cloud services juggernaut since is a great example of this recurring pattern.

My view continues to be that we are in the late innings of a current bubble. The AI Revolution differs from previous technology paradigm shifts in some significant ways, it should be noted. There are questions around whether the electrical grid will be able to bring new capacity online fast enough to meet the fast rising demands of new massive data centers. There are also growing questions whether AI related revenues will grow fast enough to justify the trillions of dollars being allocated to the AI infrastructure buildout. Both of which I have addressed in recent articles. Today, I want to highlight two other concerns investors should have around the AI Revolution.

The first is that previous technology revolutions. whether it was the buildout of the railroad, the electrical grid or the Internet Boom provided human beings new ways to leverage their skills and enhance their productivity in major ways.

They birthed new businesses and displaced others. A classic example being automobiles putting horse carriage and buggy whip manufacturers out of business. Obviously, these new technologies disrupted the labor force in significant ways. However, none of these technologies, had the potential to actually replace human intelligence. Nor did they have the potential to trigger a 'White Collar Recession'. An early sign of this could have just occurred. October saw just over 153,000 layoff announcements. The highest level for this month since 2003.

Another way this paradigm is different than previous ones has been little commented on to this point. And that is the useful life and depreciation schedules of key assets of this buildout. The train engines and track laid during the railway boom were useful for decades. The same with key components of the build out of the electrical grid. There are electrical and signal components and track throughout the NYC subway system that are older than anyone reading this column. And the 'right of ways' bequeathed to railroads in the 19th century ended up being worth fortunes.

At the time of the Internet Bust approximately 90% of 'dark fiber' laid during the Internet Boom by the likes of Global Crossing and Ciena was not in use. Fiber optic lines had been around since the 1970s, but it was emergence of Wavelength Division Multiplexing or WDM, that allowed multiple data streams to travel through a single fiber instead of just one, which was a key foundational piece of the birth of the internet.

All this unused capacity was put to good use following the Internet Bust as 'Web 2.0' spawn the likes of Meta Platforms (META) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL). Many of these assets are still in use, after long since being fully depreciated.

And that brings us to second major way the AI Revolution is different than the technology paradigms that came before it. The useful life and depreciation of the key component of this Ai infrastructure buildout, the GPU chip, currently primarily from NVIDIA.

More and more tech giants are moving to depreciating these assets over a five-to-six-year life cycle. When in reality, working in a 24/7 capacity in a data center, their useful life is much more likely to be in the two-to-three-year range. Perhaps less if Moore's Law which has governed the semiconductor industry since its birth is still applicable.

This is critical to understand as spending on AI is already accounting for a greater percentage of GDP that recent paradigm shifts.

As can be seen in the chart above, spending on AI by the tech giants has exploded. UBS recently projected that global spending on AI will come in at $375 billion in 2025 and $500 billion in 2026. The majority of this will occur in the United States.

A few years from now, primarily the tech giants could have a cumulative $2.5 trillion of capital investment in AI subject to depreciation. The majority of which will be around GPUs. Even on a five-year depreciation schedule, that is $500 billion annually in depreciation costs. Obviously, this will greatly impact EPS at the major tech giants. Something few investors are taking fully into consideration in my opinion.

That is bad enough, but if these GPUs have a useful life that turns out to be more in line with the historical two-to-three-year semiconductor replacement cycle, these tech giants will have future expenses (to replace chips) that will be much, much higher than currently factored into the market. This could cause future earnings growth projections to fall significantly if this comes to fruition. Obviously, this would have hugely negative ramifications for the overall market.

And this is just food for thought as the AI Revolution enters its fourth year.
